name: Oat (food medium)
basis: per 100 g dry oat grain (Avena sativa, whole groat/rolled, uncooked)

components.name: starch
components.source: Alemayehu 2023 doi:10.1155/2023/2730175 (starch ~85% of carbohydrate) applied to USDA FDC 169705 carbohydrate 66.27 g
components.evidence: DOMINANT carbon/energy source in oat: ~85% of the ~66 g carbohydrate is starch (25-30% amylose). Requires amylases for microbial use.

components.name: beta-glucan
components.evidence: Mixed-linkage (1,3;1,4)-beta-D-glucan, the defining soluble fibre of oat; second major fermentable polysaccharide after starch. Midpoint of reported range.

components.name: total dietary fiber
components.evidence: Total dietary fibre (includes the ~4 g beta-glucan above plus cellulose/arabinoxylan).

components.name: sucrose
components.source: USDA total sugars 0.99 g (FDC 169705); sucrose is the dominant free sugar in oat (Alemayehu 2023)
components.evidence: Principal free sugar of oat grain; oat total sugars are low (~1 g) and sucrose accounts for the majority.

components.name: D-glucose
components.source: Alemayehu 2023 doi:10.1155/2023/2730175 (free monosaccharides glucose+fructose ~small fraction of ~1 g total sugars)
components.evidence: Minor free monosaccharide; oat free sugars are dominated by sucrose, with small glucose and fructose.

components.name: D-fructose
components.evidence: Minor free monosaccharide alongside glucose; ADDED (was missing from current profile).

components.name: raffinose/fructans
components.source: Alemayehu 2023 doi:10.1155/2023/2730175 (kestose, neokestose, bifurcose, raffinose reported qualitatively)
components.evidence: Small oligosaccharide/fructan fraction present but not reliably quantified; included at null.

components.name: oxalic acid
components.source: Yadessa et al. 2021 PMC8361264 (oxalate 28.2-71.4 mg/100g DM, midpoint)
components.evidence: Main measured organic acid of oat grain; ADDED (organic acids were absent from current profile).

components.name: phytic acid
components.source: Yadessa et al. 2021 PMC8361264 (phytate 269.6-293.0 mg/100g DM, midpoint)
components.evidence: Dominant organic phosphate/acid of the grain; chelates minerals (reduces Fe/Zn bioavailability). ADDED.

components.name: citric acid
components.source: present as ubiquitous TCA intermediate in plant tissue; not quantified for oat grain in literature
components.evidence: Included at null; oat is a starch-dominated dry seed and free TCA-cycle acids are minor and largely unreported.

components.name: malic acid
components.source: ubiquitous TCA intermediate; not quantified for oat grain in literature
components.evidence: Included at null; minor in oat grain, no reliable published amount.

components.name: total protein
components.source: USDA FDC 169705 (16.89 g); literature range 11.9-15.8 g PMC8361264
components.evidence: Oat is protein-rich among cereals; globulins (avenalins) dominate.

components.name: L-glutamate
components.evidence: Most abundant amino acid in oat protein.

components.name: L-valine
components.evidence: ADDED - valine was missing from the current component list.

components.name: L-serine
components.evidence: Current list had this as D-serine; oat protein serine is the L-form.

components.name: L-lysine
components.evidence: First-limiting amino acid in oat protein.

components.name: D-glucose (starch monomer note)
components.evidence: See starch entry; free glucose amount given above.

components.name: iron
components.evidence: Fe present as Fe3+/Fe2+; oat is Fe-rich but phytate limits bioavailability.

components.name: phosphorus (phosphate)
components.evidence: Largely bound as phytate.

components.name: manganese
components.evidence: Oat is exceptionally Mn-rich (>140% DV).

components.name: selenium
components.evidence: Geographically variable (soil-dependent); ADDED amount.

components.name: biotin (B7)
components.source: USDA (oats ~73% DV biotin, DV 30 µg) via myfooddata 169705
components.evidence: ADDED amount; oat is a notable biotin source.

components.name: alpha-tocopherol (vitamin E)
components.evidence: Oat vitamin E is dominated by tocotrienols (in the oil), reported separately in some sources; alpha-tocopherol amount here.

components.name: linoleic acid (18:2 n-6)
components.source: USDA FDC 169705 (PUFA 2.535 g; linoleic dominant ~40% of fat)
components.evidence: Dominant PUFA of oat oil.

components.name: oleic acid (18:1 n-9)
components.evidence: Dominant MUFA of oat oil.

components.name: palmitic acid (16:0)
components.source: USDA FDC 169705 (saturated total 1.217 g; palmitic is main SFA)
components.evidence: Main saturated fatty acid; ADDED (was missing).

components.name: alpha-linolenic acid (18:3 n-3)
components.evidence: Minor omega-3.

components.name: dodecanoic acid (lauric, 12:0)
components.evidence: Trace saturated fatty acid; retained from original profile, unquantified.

components.name: water
components.evidence: Grain moisture.

components.name: cobalt (Co2+)
components.evidence: Ultratrace; retained as microbial B12 cofactor relevance, unquantified.

components.name: molybdenum (molybdate)
components.evidence: Ultratrace essential cofactor; retained, unquantified.

components.name: iodine
components.evidence: Ultratrace; retained, unquantified.

remove.name: Creatine cytosol
remove.reason: animal muscle metabolite, biologically implausible in a plant grain; FooDB predicted/contaminant

remove.name: Taurine C2H7NO3S
remove.reason: animal-derived sulfonic amino acid, not synthesized by cereals; FooDB predicted

remove.name: Dopamine
remove.reason: animal catecholamine neurotransmitter; not a growth nutrient of oat, trace/predicted FooDB entry

remove.name: Serotonin
remove.reason: animal indoleamine neurotransmitter; implausible as an oat nutrient, FooDB predicted

remove.name: CO2 CO2
remove.reason: medium scaffolding gas, not a food-derived nutrient (drop from composition list)

remove.name: O2 O2
remove.reason: medium scaffolding gas, not a food-derived nutrient

missing_added: L-valine (essential AA absent from current profile)
selenium amount, biotin amount, choline amount
quantitative amounts for all amino acids, minerals, vitamins, fatty acids
keep_uncovered: Avenanthramide 2c / 2p / 2f / K (oat-specific alkaloids, genuine phytochemicals, no BiGG met)
ferulic acid / caffeic acid / p-coumaric acid / sinapic acid / syringic acid (hydroxycinnamates)
secoisolariciresinol / pinoresinol / lariciresinol / syringaresinol / medioresinol / matairesinol (lignans)
scopoletin, vanillin, vanillate, 4-hydroxybenzaldehyde, quercetin (genuine oat phenolics)
putrescine / spermidine / spermine (polyamines; genuine in oat, no BiGG growth role)
glycine betaine, choline (osmolytes; choline quantified above)
sources: USDA FoodData Central FDC 169705 (Oats, SR Legacy) — proximates, amino acids, minerals, vitamins, fatty-acid classes
USDA / nutritionvalue.org NDB 57602100 (Oats, raw) — selenium, vitamin E, vitamin K, total sugars
Yadessa et al. 2021, PMC8361264 — oxalate, phytate, tannin, mineral ranges of oat grain
Alemayehu et al. 2023, The Scientific World Journal doi:10.1155/2023/2730175 — starch fraction, free-sugar and beta-glucan composition
notes: Oat is a starch-dominated dry cereal grain: the overwhelming carbon/energy source is starch (~58 g/100g, ~85% of carbohydrate), followed by beta-glucan (~4 g) and other fibre; free sugars are low (~1 g, mostly sucrose). Unlike fruit foods, oat has minimal free organic acids — oxalic and phytic acid are the only well-quantified ones. Protein is high (~17 g) and glutamate-rich. Removed four animal-derived metabolites (creatine, taurine, dopamine, serotonin) as biologically implausible FooDB noise; added valine, fructose, palmitic acid, beta-glucan, quantified starch, and the organic acids.
